content.problemStatement:
Chef lives in an N×MN \times MN×M grid. He is currently participating in a treasure hunt, and has two items left to find. Chef knows that the Manhattan distance between the cells containing these two items is exactly kkk. He wants to know, in how many different pairs of cells can the two items be present?

Let AkA_kAk​ be the number of desired pairs when the value of Manhattan distance between the two cells containing these two items is equal to kkk. Let C=∑i=1N+M−2Ai⋅31i−1C = \sum_{i=1}^{N + M - 2} A_i \cdot 31^{i-1}C=∑i=1N+M−2​Ai​⋅31i−1. You have to find the value of CCC.

The answer may be large, so you need to find it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.

The Manhattan distance between two points (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) and (x′,y′)(x', y')(x′,y′) is defined as ∣x−x′∣+∣y−y′∣|x - x'| + |y - y'|∣x−x′∣+∣y−y′∣.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each testcase contains of a single line of input, two integers NNN and MMM.


Output Format
On a new line for each test case, print CCC modulo 998244353998244353998244353
constraints: The sum of NNN over all tests does not exceed 10710^7107.
The sum of MMM over all tests does not exceed 10710^7107.
The sum of NNN over all tests does not exceed 10410^4104.
The sum of MMM over all tests does not exceed 10410^4104.
The sum of NNN over all tests does not exceed 10610^6106.
The sum of MMM over all tests does not exceed 10610^6106.
